blocked_on: EXP-254 must be integrated first (this addendum's insertion anchor is <details id="p4-427">, which EXP-254 introduces).
replacement_html:
4.2.7a Station-by-station coverage under dome geometry

Section 4.2.7 closes with a trilemma; this addendum quantifies its "Borrowed" branch. Projecting the dome's nine published eclipse stations onto the disc and asking which fall inside the dome-geometry penumbra band — 85 km wide, traced by the Moon's orbital ground track during the ~3 h eclipse window — yields zero. Even the closest station (Ebro) sits 444 km from the projected umbra path, ten times the penumbra radius; the most distant (Canary Islands) sits 4,084 km away. The dome's own parameters require seven to nine of its nine named stations to record exactly zero magnetic eclipse signal.

The trilemma in 4.2.7 argues qualitatively that the dome's per-station eclipse predictions can only be generated by borrowing globe geometry. Here we make the argument quantitative: under the dome's own parameters, the published nine-station list is geometrically incoherent.

The dome's published 9-station list

The eclipse page assigns specific coverage and field-strength magnitudes to nine European magnetic observatories (raw text mirror at raw-text/03-predictions.txt):

IDObservatoryCodeCountryLatLonDome coverageFSF
E001EbroEBRSpain40.96° N0.33° E0.951.029
E002San PabloSPTSpain39.55° N4.35° W0.901.001
E003EskdalemuirESKScotland55.32° N3.20° W0.551.722
E004LerwickLERScotland60.13° N1.18° W0.422.075
E005HartlandHADEngland51.00° N4.48° W0.801.471
E006NiemegkNGKGermany52.07° N12.68° E0.401.529
E007Canary IslandsSNKSpain28.32° N16.43° W0.720.642
E008ChambonCLFFrance48.03° N2.26° E0.701.325
E009CoimbraCOIPortugal40.22° N8.42° W0.921.008

The latitudes span 28.3° N (Canary Islands) to 60.1° N (Lerwick) — a range of 31.8°, or roughly 3,500 km of north–south spread on the globe. The longitudes span 16.4° W (Canary) to 12.7° E (Niemegk) — about 29° of east–west spread. The dome assigns each station a specific coverage value and a specific field-strength factor; no station is rounded to zero.

Dome geometric facts (carried from 4.2.7)

From 4.2.7: at hSun= 5,733 km, hMoon= 2,534 km, the NASA Besselian angular sizes for August 12, 2026 force a totality (umbra) ground radius of 0.66 km and a penumbra ground radius of Rp ≈ 42 km — i.e. an 85-km-wide annular band around the Moon's instantaneous projected position. The Moon's lateral ground velocity is at most 87 km/min (a 24-h circuit of the 20,015-km disc rim), or about 25 km/min mid-disc as a charity. The eclipse window is ~3 hours from first to last umbra contact. Therefore the longest possible disc arc the Moon's ground projection can trace during the window is 87 × 180 = 15,660 km at the upper bound, or 4,500 km under mid-disc charity — and the penumbra band along that arc is 85 km wide.

Disc projection of the 9 stations

To compare station positions to the Moon's transit arc on the disc we must place both on the same map. Standard flat-earth cartography uses azimuthal-equidistant projection from the North Pole, with the disc rim at r = π·Rglobe = 20,015 km — exactly the dome's published rim radius. Under this projection a globe point at (lat φ, lon λ) maps to disc polar (r, θ) where r = (90° − φ) · Rdisc / 90° and θ = λ. The dome does not publish a different projection function, so this is the most charitable choice; any alternative consistent with the disc rim produces inter-station separations of the same order.

The nine stations' disc radii, sorted: Lerwick 6,642 km, Eskdalemuir 7,713 km, Niemegk 8,435 km, Hartland 8,674 km, Chambon 9,335 km, Ebro 10,907 km, Coimbra 11,070 km, San Pablo 11,220 km, Canary Islands 13,717 km. The radial spread is 7,075 km — the stations are NOT clustered at one disc-radius.

Per-station distance from the projected umbra path

NASA's published umbra central line for August 12, 2026 (greatest eclipse 17:46:13 UT at 65.0° N, 25.2° W) was sampled at fifteen ~5-minute intervals from first umbra contact (≈ 17:09 UT, Siberian Arctic) to last umbra contact (≈ 18:21 UT, Spain) and projected through the same AE-from-N map. For each station we compute the minimum Euclidean distance to that polyline:

StationDisc r (km)Min distance to projected umbra path (km)Multiple of RpInside 85-km band?
E001 Ebro10,90744410.6×NO
E002 San Pablo11,22053312.7×NO
E005 Hartland8,67471016.9×NO
E009 Coimbra11,07099323.6×NO
E003 Eskdalemuir7,7131,21729.0×NO
E008 Chambon9,3351,51436.1×NO
E004 Lerwick6,6421,80843.0×NO
E006 Niemegk8,4353,26777.8×NO
E007 Canary Islands13,7174,08497.2×NO

Figure. The dome's nine eclipse stations on the disc, with the Aug 12 2026 NASA umbra central line projected onto the disc using the same AE-from-N map. Station markers show min-distance to the projected path. Closest: Ebro at 444 km — ten penumbra-radii away. Furthest: Canary Islands at 4,084 km — ninety-seven penumbra-radii away.

Zero of nine stations lie within the 42-km penumbra radius. Zero of nine lie within the 85-km band. To touch all nine stations from any single 3-hour disc arc, the penumbra ground radius would have to inflate to at least 4,084 km — corresponding (via Rp ∝ hMoon) to a Moon altitude near 246,000 km, contradicting the dome's published 2,534 km figure by a factor of 97 and inflating the Moon's apparent diameter by the same factor.

Even more constrained: constant-r orbital reading

The above charity already grants the dome a non-circular Moon orbit. The dome's actual published orbital geometry has the Moon at fixed altitude 2,534 km, presumably orbiting the disc center at constant orbital radius — its ground projection then traces a circle of constant r, not a curve crossing many disc-radii. Under that strict reading, only stations whose disc-radius matches the Moon's orbital radius (within 84 km, the band width) can ever be in the penumbra. The closest pair of stations in disc-radius is Coimbra–San Pablo at Δr = 150 km — already outside an 85-km window. Under the strict constant-r orbital reading, no two of the nine stations can be in the penumbra band simultaneously, and at most one can be in the band at all. The remaining eight must record exactly zero.

Pre-empting the time-distribution rebuttal

A defender might argue that the nine stations are read sequentially, not simultaneously: the Moon transits over hours, so different stations enter and leave the band at different times. The numbers do not allow this rescue. The eclipse window is 3 h, not 24 h; over those 3 h the Moon's ground projection traces at most 15,660 km of arc at upper-bound rim speed, or about 4,500 km mid-disc. The 85-km-wide band along that arc covers between 0.4 million and 1.3 million km² — roughly 0.1 % of the 1.26 billion km² disc surface. A nine-station nearest-neighbour tour through the dome's published list is 16,439 km long on the disc — already longer than the maximum possible arc — and even that tour would require the band to be 442 km wide (not 85 km) to reach Lerwick. The arithmetic does not get rescued by giving the Moon more time, because the Moon does not have more time during the eclipse window.

Falsification

The dome's own parameters predict a measurable eclipse signal at zero of its nine named stations under the most charitable disc-projection reading, and at most one under its strict constant-r orbital geometry. The remaining eight or nine stations must record zero. Yet the dome publishes per-station coverage between 0.40 and 0.95 — implying meaningful magnetic anomaly signal — for all nine. The published predictions cannot simultaneously be V51-internal evidence and respect V51's own occultation geometry. Either:

  1. The 2,534 km Moon altitude is wrong (by ~97×), invalidating the V51 parameter set;
  2. The station selection was imported from globe geometry — picking the nine European INTERMAGNET observatories that lie on or near the standard-physics path of partial eclipse — confirming the "Borrowed" branch of 4.2.7; or
  3. The mechanism is not occultation at all, in which case localization to those specific nine stations needs an independent dome-internal explanation that the dome page does not provide.

This is the same trilemma 4.2.7 establishes structurally. The station-by-station math turns the trilemma's "Borrowed" branch from a qualitative observation ("globe physics chooses the stations, then the dome attaches its labels") into a quantitative kill: the dome's own parameters produce a 42-km penumbra radius and the stations sit 444–4,084 km from the path. There is no charitable reading in which the dome's 9-station list survives its own geometry.

The dome-geometry penumbra band (85 km wide) is too thin to render at this scale; an inset shows it at 50x magnification at the path-Ebro tangent point. Under dome geometry, all 9 stations must record zero magnetic eclipse signal.</desc>

math_verification.claim: Penumbra ground radius R_p = 42 km under dome geometry
math_verification.verified: INHERITED from EXP-254 math_verification point 5: R_p = R_M + (R_S + R_M)·h_moon/D = 12.0 + 38.3·2534/3199 = 42.4 km. Curmudgeon-verified in EXP-254 review.

math_verification.claim: Disc projection AE-from-N with R_disc = 20015 km
math_verification.verified: Standard flat-earth cartography. R_disc = π · R_globe = π · 6371 km = 20,015 km, matching the dome's published rim radius exactly. Verified: (90-lat)*20015/90 maps north pole to r=0 and equator to r=20015. Sanity check: at 41° N (Spain), r = (90-41)*20015/90 = 49*222.4 = 10,899 km, consistent with computed values in table.

Public INTERMAGNET observatory positions: EBR 40.957/0.333, SPT 39.547/-4.349, ESK 55.317/-3.200, LER 60.133/-1.183, HAD 50.995/-4.483, NGK 52.072/12.675, GUI/Canary 28.32/-16.43, CLF 48.025/2.260, COI 40.222/-8.420. Note: dome lists code 'SNK' for the Canary station; standard INTERMAGNET code is 'GUI' (Güímar). We use the Güímar position as the closest plausible match.

math_verification.claim: NASA Aug 12 2026 path central line sample
math_verification.verified: Sampled from NASA GSFC Five Millennium Catalog page for 2026 Aug 12. Greatest eclipse 17:46:13 UT at 65.0°N, 25.2°W (Besselian elements pinned in EXP-254). Path crosses Russian Arctic (~17:09 first umbra contact), polar region, Greenland, Iceland, North Atlantic, NW Spain (~18:21 last umbra contact). 15-point ~5-min sampling captures the central-line arc faithfully for distance computation; finer sampling does not change min-distance results to within 1%.

Computed by Python script (outputs/eclipse-analysis/compute.py) using point-to-segment distance against the projected polyline. Closest point for Ebro: end-of-path at (10890, -380), distance sqrt((10907-10890)^2 + (63-(-380))^2) = sqrt(256 + 196249) = 443.3 km. Hand-verified.

math_verification.claim: Constant-r orbital reading: closest pair Coimbra-San Pablo at dr=150 km
math_verification.verified: From sorted r_disc list: Coimbra 11,070 / San Pablo 11,220, dr=150. Next-closest: Ebro 10,907 / Coimbra 11,070, dr=164. Both above the 84-km band threshold (penumbra full width). Therefore at most ONE station can be in the band at any time under constant-r orbital geometry.

math_verification.claim: Inflated R_p of 4084 km implies h_moon ~ 246,000 km
math_verification.verified: Penumbra radius scales linearly with h_moon for fixed angular sizes (R_p = R_M + (R_S + R_M)·h_M/D, both h_S and h_M scale together). Approximation: h_moon needed = 2534 · (4084 / 42) = 246,415 km. Order of magnitude correct; the point of the calculation is to show the required altitude is astronomical (not dome-compatible).

Computed via greedy nearest-neighbour heuristic in compute.py. Greedy NN gives an upper bound on optimal TSP tour length; even this upper bound exceeds 15,660 km (3-hour arc at 87 km/min upper bound), so even an optimal touring path cannot be traced by the Moon during the eclipse window.

holes_addressed: EXP-254 trilemma's 'Borrowed' branch is qualitative; this addendum quantifies it with per-station distances
Defender's escape via 'maybe each station is read at a different time during the 3-hour window' — addressed in time-distribution rebuttal section with arc-length math
Defender's escape via 'maybe the projection is wrong' — addressed by anchoring projection to dome's own published 20,015 km rim and noting any consistent alternative produces same-order separations
new_evidence_added: 9-station per-station table with disc-projected coordinates and min-distance to projected umbra path
Constant-r orbital reading: AT MOST 1 station can be in penumbra band; closest pair is 150 km apart in disc radius (vs. 85 km band)
Inflated-R_p calculation: full coverage requires R_p~4084 km, equivalent to Moon altitude ~246,000 km (97x dome value)
Nearest-neighbour TSP tour 16,439 km > 15,660 km maximum arc length

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome doesn't use AE-from-N projection.
anticipated_objections.response: The dome's published R_disc = 20,015 km is exactly π · R_globe, the exact value AE-from-N gives for the disc rim. Any alternative projection consistent with that rim produces inter-station separations of the same order; AE is the most charitable single choice.

anticipated_objections.objection: Stations are read at different times across the 3-hour window, not simultaneously.
anticipated_objections.response: Addressed in pre-empting section. Maximum 3-hour arc length is 15,660 km at 87 km/min upper bound; nearest-neighbour TSP tour is 16,439 km. Even an optimally-routed Moon cannot trace a path through all 9 stations in 3 hours, and the band is 85 km wide regardless.

anticipated_objections.objection: Maybe the dome's Moon orbital radius can vary, allowing radial drift across the eclipse window.
anticipated_objections.response: Conceded as the most charitable reading; that reading IS the per-station-min-distance calculation against the projected NASA path. Even with full radial freedom, all 9 stations are 444-4084 km from the path. The constant-r reading is even more constraining (max 1 station).

anticipated_objections.objection: The published 'coverage' values may not mean penumbra-band membership; they could be interpreted as ratios of some other quantity.
anticipated_objections.response: The dome's own page presents coverage as solar disc obscuration fraction (0 = no eclipse seen, 1 = total). FSF is a magnetic field-strength multiplier. Both presuppose the station is in the path of the Moon's geometric shadow. If 'coverage' means something else, the dome must publish the alternative meaning; until it does, the standard astronomical interpretation applies.

anticipated_objections.objection: The Canary Islands code SNK doesn't match standard INTERMAGNET (GUI/Güímar).
anticipated_objections.response: Acknowledged. We use the Güímar position 28.32°N, 16.43°W as the closest plausible match. Even if SNK refers to a different Canary station, the disc-radius is dominated by the latitude (28°N), giving r~13,717 km — far from any other station regardless of exact longitude. The kill argument is robust to ±1° position uncertainty on this station.

kernel_of_truth: The dome got the COVERAGE values right — they match the standard-physics partial-eclipse percentages the stations will actually observe on Aug 12 2026. That correctness is exactly what makes the argument bite: the only way to publish accurate coverage figures for all nine stations is to compute them from globe geometry (distant Sun, distant Moon, spherical Earth), which is the V52 model the dome rejects. The dome's predictions therefore work BECAUSE they import globe physics — not despite it.
why_kernel_doesnt_save_claim: Importing globe-physics coverage values cannot rescue dome-physics localization. Even granted accurate coverages, the dome's own 2,534 km Moon and 5,733 km Sun produce a 42-km penumbra radius that does not reach any of the nine stations. The dome can publish accurate coverages or self-consistent geometry; it cannot publish both.
cross_references: p4-424 (FSF tan(latitude) tautology — supports the 'station selection borrowed' branch)

fact_verification.R_disc: 20015 km (dome's published value, equals π * 6371 km)
fact_verification.eclipse_window: ~3 hours (first umbra contact ~17:09 UT to last contact ~18:21 UT, NASA)

files_to_commit: data/sections.json (insert new <details id="p4-427-station-coverage"> after p4-427 closes inside p4 section)
decider_instructions: 1) Wait for EXP-254 integration first — this addendum's insertion anchor (<details id="p4-427">) only exists after EXP-254 lands. 2) After EXP-254 is in main, copy outputs/eclipse-analysis/eclipse-stations-vs-penumbra-band.svg to docs/assets/. 3) Insert the replacement_html block in data/sections.json part4.html immediately after the closing </details> of p4-427 and before the next p4 sibling. 4) Run tests/run.sh and node test.js. 5) Route to curmudgeon for math verification before commit per operator note (priority high but not emergency).
curmudgeon_review_focus: Verify the AE-from-N projection: r=(90-lat)*20015/90 is correctly applied for all 9 stations
Spot-check at least 2 station min-distances against the projected NASA path (table values)
Verify the constant-r orbital reading: closest pair Coimbra-San Pablo Δr=150 km > 85-km band
Verify the inflated-R_p calculation: 2534 * 4084/42 ≈ 246,000 km — order of magnitude correct
Sanity-check the time-distribution rebuttal: 87 km/min * 180 min = 15,660 km arc; nearest-neighbour tour = 16,439 km > 15,660 km
SVG accuracy: stations plotted at correct disc coordinates; path projection visually consistent with table values
build_impact: +1 new <details> section in part4 (~960 words HTML); +1 SVG asset (~7 KB). No changes to existing sections.
acceptance_criteria: Per-station table with 9 rows and disc-distance from Moon transit arc — DELIVERED
Concrete count: of 9 stations, exactly K can be in the dome's penumbra band; K computed not asserted — DELIVERED (K=0 under arc-following charity, K=1 under constant-r orbital reading)
Closing sentence quantifies the falsification (the dome's own parameters require 8-9 of 9 stations to record zero) — DELIVERED
Pre-empts altitude charity (uses observed 5,733 km / 2,534 km) — DELIVERED
Pre-empts velocity charity (uses 87 km/min upper and 25 km/min mid-disc) — DELIVERED
Cross-references EXP-254 as parent — DELIVERED (anchor link to p4-427)
SVG: 9 stations + projected penumbra band — DELIVERED (eclipse-stations-vs-penumbra-band.svg)
out_of_scope_explicit: Re-deriving R_p from first principles — already done in EXP-254
Critiquing the dome's coverage formula vs FSF formula — covered in p4-424 (FSF tan-tautology)
Predicting what the dome will publish if it patches around this finding — not our job; we describe the geometry and let the reader judge
newsworthy: Dome's own parameters require 8 of its 9 named eclipse stations to record zero — yet it publishes signal at all 9.
notes_for_decider: Operator priority HIGH per HNOTE. Operator considers this 'the strongest single move available against the dome's headline eclipse-prediction claim'. Math is mechanical and reproducible from compute.py in outputs/eclipse-analysis/. Both the JSON math verification and the in-prose numbers were generated from the same script.
